food_subgroup: Rice bowl dish — steamed Japanese short-grain rice topped with thinly sliced simmered beef short plate and caramelised onion in sweet soy sauce (tare)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Gyudon as a prepared restaurant dish. Closest parent would be a generic rice bowl with meat topping; no exact FoodOn term available as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice bowl dish with beef and sauce topping (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 term for Gyudon)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0JKH used as closest parent for a rice bowl with beef topping. No exact Gyudon-specific FoodEx2 term available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Gyudon as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Gyudon bowl as served at Yoshinoya Singapore, comprising approximately 250g steamed Japanese short-grain rice, 100g thinly sliced simmered beef short plate with caramelised onion, and approximately 30g sweet soy sauce (tare), totalling approximately 380g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, short plate, raw (FDC ID 174034); USDA SR Legacy — White rice, short-grain, cooked (FDC ID 169756); composite dish estimation for Gyudon
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Gyudon as a composite restaurant dish. Per-100g values are derived from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked Japanese short-grain rice (FDC 169756), beef short plate (FDC 174034), caramelised onion, and sweet soy sauce tare, in proportions consistent with the Yoshinoya Singapore standard bowl (250g rice : 100g beef-onion topping : 30g sauce per 380g bowl). Macros cross-checked against Singapore HPB Foodo database for comparable rice bowl dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — Japanese short-grain rice (GI ~65–68)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 65 assigned based on University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked Japanese short-grain rice (GI range 62–68; higher amylopectin content than long-grain jasmine rice). Gyudon as a composite dish has no direct GI measurement in approved sources. The rice component dominates the glycaemic response (~250g of 380g total). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 72.2) / 100 ≈ 46.9; rounded to 44.9 using 69g net carbohydrate (excluding fibre). Protein and fat from beef short plate moderately attenuate postprandial glucose rise.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 901mg sodium per standard 380g bowl (~45% of WHO 2g daily limit), primarily from sweet soy sauce tare and simmering broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Gyudon to occasional consumption and request reduced-sauce options where available, as one standard bowl provides approximately 45% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load — GI ~65 (medium), GL ~45 per standard bowl; 72g total carbohydrate per bowl including ~12g added sugar from tare sauce exceeds recommended single-meal carbohydrate targets for most T2DM patients
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should request a half-rice or small-rice portion at Yoshinoya Singapore to reduce the glycaemic load to approximately 22–24, and should account for the 12g added sugar from the tare sauce when calculating total meal carbohydrate intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef short plate (~8g per bowl) and high sodium (~901mg per bowl); beef short plate is a high-fat cut with approximately 2.1g saturated fat per 100g of composite dish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Gyudon frequency due to the combination of saturated fat (~8g per bowl from beef short plate) and high sodium (~901mg per bowl), and should consider leaner beef cuts or reduced-portion options when available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gyudon (牛丼, Japanese Beef Rice Bowl) is a classic Japanese fast-food dish consisting of steamed Japanese short-grain rice topped with thinly sliced beef short plate and caramelised onion simmered in a sweet soy sauce called tare, made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. The dish is served hot in a bowl and is the signature offering of chains such as Yoshinoya and Sukiya, both of which operate in Singapore. The rice is sticky and slightly sweet, the beef is tender and richly flavoured from the simmering sauce, and the onion adds a soft, caramelised sweetness. A standard Yoshinoya Singapore bowl weighs approximately 380g (250g rice, 100g beef-onion topping, 30g sauce) and provides approximately 610 kcal, 24g protein, 20g fat, and 72g carbohydrate. Sodium content is high at approximately 901mg per bowl. The glycaemic index is approximately 65 (medium) but the glycaemic load per bowl is approximately 45 (high), making it a high-GL meal. The dish is dairy-free but is not halal-certified at most Singapore outlets as the dashi broth may contain katsuobushi (bonito flakes). Nutritional data derived from USDA FoodData Central composite estimation for cooked Japanese short-grain rice (FDC 169756) and beef short plate (FDC 174034). Gyudon is widely available at Yoshinoya Singapore, Don Don Donki, and Pepper Lunch outlets across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Gyudon as a composite restaurant dish. Per-100g values are derived from a weighted USDA FoodData Central composite of cooked Japanese short-grain rice (FDC 169756) and beef short plate (FDC 174034) in Yoshinoya Singapore standard proportions. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database values for Japanese short-grain rice as the dominant carbohydrate component. Confidence is medium because the composite estimation introduces uncertainty in the sauce and seasoning contributions to sodium and sugar.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the Gyudon composite dish (rice + beef short plate + sweet soy tare) is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of April 2026. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in available approved sources. 3. Per-100g values represent the composite dish at the standard Yoshinoya Singapore bowl ratio (250g rice : 100g beef-onion topping : 30g sauce per 380g bowl). 4. The input source cited 'Japan MEXT 8th Ed' which is not on the approved source list; values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central (FDC 174034 for beef short plate; FDC 169756 for cooked short-grain rice) as the approved equivalent. 5. The dish is not halal-certified at most Singapore Yoshinoya outlets; dashi broth may contain katsuobushi (bonito flakes). 6. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese short-grain rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Gyudon exists in approved sources. 7. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (65 × 69g net carbohydrate per 380g bowl) / 100 ≈ 44.9.
